---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: center, middle, inverse

.size80[**Data Analysis in Political Science**]

.size50[(Spring 2024)]

.size70[
<br>
]

.size40[Justin Leinaweaver, PhD]

???

### Prep for Class
1. *Bring rulers to class*

<br>



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size90[**Introductions**]

.size65[
1. Name

2. Year in school

3. Major(s)
]

???

Let's go around the room for introductions.

I'll go first.

1. I'm Dr. Justin Leinaweaver.

2. FSU undergrad, masters in IR at UCD and PhD at Trinity.
  + My eleventh year at Drury

3. I'm a political scientist
  + Research interests: international politics, environmental politics and bargaining / negotiations AND quantitative methods

<br>

### Your turn!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size90[**Read the Syllabus!**]]

.size50[
- Components of the grade,
- Attendance policy,
- Participation points,
- Daily plan / readings,
- Buy the Wheelan book,
- etc.
]

???

Please make sure to read the syllabus before class on Friday.

<br>

We'll kick off class with any questions or concerns you have AFTER reviewing the structure of the class in the syllabus.

- Make sure you're particularly clear on these components.

- Friday will be your chance to raise questions or concerns before I assume everybody has agreed to it!

### Make sense?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-monkey_darts_politics.jpg')
background-size: 83%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

Welcome to Data Analysis in Political Science!

<br>

Hopefully, over your last year or so of poli sci classes you have come to learn that:

1. The overarching goal of political science is to better understand how the social world works,
    - Why do people do the things they do?
    - How do societies decide to make and enforce rules?
    - etc.

2. The "science" in political science means we we test our ideas in verifiable ways using real-world observations, and

3. Translating the real world into data requires measurement
    - This is one of the focuses of this class!

<br>

SLIDE: Given this focus, let's kick off our first class today with a simple measurement warm-up!





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size90[**Warm-up Exercise**]

<br>

.size70[
What is the order of students in our class by height (shortest to tallest)?
]

???

To answer this question I will ask you to stand up and organize yourselves into a line by height (from the shortest to the tallest)

I brought rulers if you need them!

*Let them work on this on their own*

<br>

### Ok, ready to present your work?

<br>

### 1. Have you answered my question?

<br>

### 2. Please describe for me how you completed this task. Be specific.

<br>

### 3. What were the biggest source or sources of uncertainty in this task?

<br>

### 4. How confident are you in your answer? 

#### Put differently, is there any situation where you would be uncomfortable relying on this answer?

<br>

Let's say the government decided to provide college students with money for school indexed to height.

### Are you still happy with this answer given that purpose? Why or why not?

<br>

Ok, good! Next...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size90[**Warm-up Exercise (v2)**]

<br>

.size70[
What is the average height of the students in our class to the nearest millimeter?
]

???

*Let them work on this on their own*

<br>

### Ok, ready to present your work?

<br>

### 1. Have you answered my question?

<br>

### 2. Please describe for me how you completed this task. Be specific.

<br>

### 3. What were the biggest source or sources of uncertainty in this task?

<br>

### 4. How confident are you in your answer? 

#### Put differently, is there any situation where you would be uncomfortable relying on this answer?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size90[**Warm-up Exercises**]

.size60[
1. What is the order of students in our class by height?

2. What is the average height of our class?
]

???

### Ok, what do we learn from our warm-up measurement exercises?

<br>

Translating the real world into data ALWAYS carries uncertainty.

- Even for something as concrete as "height" we have to remember that...

- SLIDES x 4: Subject, technique, tool and validation


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_subject.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right
class: slideblue

.left[.size60[

+ **Subject**

+ .textgrey[Tool]

+ .textgrey[Process]

+ .textgrey[Validation]
]]

???

Measurements are only as certain as the definitions we begin with.

- Without a clear definition of the concept we are measuring, the measurements are basically meaningless.

<br>

Measuring height means deciding what features of a person are height and what aren't (e.g. hair vs not).

- Funnily enough, there isn't a "right" answer necessarily. 

- The goal is a clear and defensible answer.

<br>

So, even if "height" means different things to different people, you as the researcher make clear what you are defining for this study.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_rulers.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right
class: slideblue

.left[.size60[

+ .textgrey[Subject]

+ **Tool**

+ .textgrey[Process]

+ .textgrey[Validation]
]]

???

Measurements are only as certain as the tools we build to make them.

- A "useful" tool is one that validly captures the key elements of your definition.

<br>

In our height example:

- Are the markings clear?
- Do they line up with the ends of the ruler well?
- Are they long enough for the job at hand?
- Do they change with use (e.g. warp)?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_technique.jpg')
background-size: 50%
background-position: right
class: slideblue

.left[.size60[

+ .textgrey[Subject]

+ .textgrey[Tool]

+ **Process**

+ .textgrey[Validation]
]]

???

Measurements are only as certain as the process is clearly laid out and rigorously followed to make them.

- A "reliable" process is one that generates the same results each time it is used.

<br>

In terms of our height measurements:

- Did everyone agree on a specific height measuring process?

- Did everyone follow it perfectly?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_calibration.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right
class: slideblue

.left[.size60[

+ .textgrey[Subject]

+ .textgrey[Tool]

+ .textgrey[Process]

+ **Validation**
]]

???

As a bonus, measurements are most certain when they are consistent with other attempts to measure the same concept.

<br>

Again, for the height exercise:

- Did the manufacturer of the ruler tie their work to an official or external authoritative source? Have the ruler length's been checked?

- Did you check each others' work when measuring?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size90[**Warm-up Exercises**]

.size60[
1. What is the order of students in our class by height?

2. What is the average height of our class?
]

???

Bottom line time!

### What does all this mean for our ability to answer questions with data? What do we have to keep in mind as social scientists?

<br>

Every measure is uncertain.

- Our job is to find or create measures that are precise enough to answer the questions we are asking.

<br>

You clearly have a good enough sense of your height to navigate most parts of your life (e.g. can I fit in this room? Do I need bigger pants?)

- Even a cheap wooden 12" ruler will let us order the class by height! That's the point.

<br>

HOWEVER, a precise class average to a small degree of error will require WAY more effort to produce.

- Key if you're trying to launch a space ship, create fusion or argue that raising the minimum wage will raise or lower unemployment!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size70[**Useful measurements must:**]

.size50[
1. Define the Concept

2. Specify the Tool

3. Specify the Process

4. Test for Robustness
]


???

Bottom line, useful measurements must always:

- Define the key concept (e.g. what is human height?)

- Specify the tool used (e.g. ruler type, size, material),

- Specify the process employed (e.g. how to stand, where, ruler held, etc.), and

- Consider how it can be validated.

### Make sense?

### Write this down!

<br>

Good, let's try to measure something harder!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size65[**Assignment for Friday**]]

.size50[
1. What was the population of the United States of America in 2020?

2. How many countries are there in the world today?

3. Is the US currently at war with Russia?
]

???

Here's your first assignment for the class.

#### To earn your first participation point:

- Due **BEFORE CLASS** on Friday
- Submit to our discussion board on **Canvas**.
- I will give you the rest of class today to work on this.

<br>

### Are the questions clear? Do you understand what I'm asking?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**Assignment for Friday**]]

.size45[
1. Population of the USA in 2020?

2. Number of countries in the world today?

3. Is the US currently at war with Russia?

.center[**Measurement Elements:**] 
.center[Definitions, Tools, Process and Validation]
]

???

To clarify: I'm not asking you to find and adopt someone else's answer.

- This isn't a simple "let me Google that for you" exercise.

<br>

Your job is to think critically about how each of these questions is a significant measurement challenge.

- Think carefully about how an answer to each question depends on the elements we've discussed today: **definitions, tools, process and validation.**

### Make sense?

<br>

- SLIDE: A complete answer requires...




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[
1. Population of the USA in 2020?
2. Number of countries in the world today?
3. Is the US currently at war with Russia?

**The Assignment Requires:**
- A specific answer, 
- the source(s) used (APA), and 
- an argument, focused on measurement, that your answer is useful (3+ sentences).
]

???

For each question you must provide:

1. A specific answer in a complete sentence, 

2. The source in APA format (see the Purdue OWL for guidance), and

3. An argument that this is a useful answer **focused on the measurement and the data**
    - **e.g. explain how you used the source(s) to define the concept, select a tool or accept the process they followed and why you are confident in it**
    - Do not rely on claims of authority (e.g. they are experts), our job is to evaluate the actual measures
    - 3-5 sentences

<br>

### Questions?

The discussion board on canvas will stop accepting entries when class starts on Friday.

This means the point depends on submitting in time!

Let's get to it!
